fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to sort out the feature flag system for the web app. Basically right now devs are copy-pasting if/else checks all over the place — in views, templates, URL configs — and every time we want to roll something out to a subset of users or kill a feature during an incident we have to do a full deploy. It's a mess and things keep drifting out of sync between layers.

We need a clean, centralized way to toggle behaviors on/off at runtime without touching code. Flags should be evaluatable against things like the current user, URL path, query params, date/time, and so on — similar to how we handled the conditional login stuff last sprint, but more generalized. The system should work across views, URL routing, and templates consistently.

There should also be a way to pull flag definitions from multiple places — both static config and whatever storage backend we're using — and merge them. Validation, admin forms, caching per request, all of that needs to be covered. If a flag isn't defined at all, that should be distinguishable from a flag that's explicitly turned off — that distinction has burned us before.

Also the code structure matters here, this shouldn't end up as one giant file. Keep things separated by responsibility. Let's make sure edge cases like missing context, bad config, and broken providers are handled gracefully and don't just blow up.

One other important detail on the state behavior: if a flag just doesn’t exist in config anywhere, that needs to come back as its own third state, `state=none`, not get flattened into `state=false`. We really do need callers to be able to tell “explicitly off” from “never defined.” For the convenience helpers, the enabled helper should still treat none as off, so `enabled=false`, and the disabled helper should treat none as on, so `disabled=true`.

On the request-dependent checks, when something in the condition layer needs request context and there isn’t a request available, we should normalize that result instead of blowing up. The shape needs to be exactly three fields: `error=missing_required_argument`, `argument=request`, and `condition=<condition_name>`. Same idea for a class-based view guard with no flag name configured — that should come back as `error=misconfigured` and `reason=flag_name_required`, not an unhandled exception. And if a flag-guarded route gets built with something that’s neither a callable nor a valid route group, return `error=invalid_view` rather than throwing.

A couple notes on caching and call-time inputs too. Once middleware has cached the flag collection on a request, every later check for that same request should use that cached snapshot even if live config changes after that. If a request never went through the middleware, then it should reflect the current live configuration. Also, that cached attribute should only exist after middleware runs. On the template side, both the Django template tags (`flag_enabled`, `flag_disabled`) and the Jinja2 globals should accept an extra keyword argument at call time and pass it through into condition evaluation, which is how the value-matching conditions get the value they need.

For admin/config plumbing, the form’s condition choices need to be calculated when the form instance is created, not once at module import time. That way if a condition gets registered later, a newly created form still sees it as a valid option. Validation should scan all configured flags for conditions that point at unregistered condition types. A clean config should report `warnings=0`, and if a flag references an unknown condition type it should produce exactly one warning entry with the stable diagnostic id `warning id=flags.E001`.

Also, for persisted records coming from the `DatabaseFlagsSource` provider in `flags/sources.py`, each record is still just the persisted flag data `(name, condition, value)` from the database-like store, and it needs to render as a readable sentence in exactly this form: `description=<FLAG_NAME> is enabled when <condition> is <value>`. All three pieces are required. And just to anchor the implementation shape we’re talking about, the condition evaluation subsystem in `flags/conditions.py` is still the named condition class pattern with a `check(value, request)` interface used for user, anonymous, parameter, path, and date conditions.

A few exact details to be precise about: A flag that is not configured at all must resolve to a distinct third state — `state=none` — not the same as `state=false`. This is critical so callers can tell 'explicitly turned off' from 'never defined'. The enabled helper treats none as off (enabled=false), and the disabled helper treats none as on (disabled=true).

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: resolve_flag(flag_name: str, mode: str, request: Optional[dict] = None) -> str

trigger_keywords: boolean condition string parsing
how is true evaluated in boolean condition
case insensitive true string
what values evaluate to on in boolean condition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The boolean condition evaluates on for the literal boolean `true` and for any case-insensitive spelling of the string 'true' (e.g. 'True', 'TRUE', 'true'). Every other value — including 'False', 'false', the literal false, or any other string — evaluates off.

trigger_keywords: parameter condition exact match
query string condition value check
what value must query param be for parameter condition
parameter condition True vs true
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parameter condition passes only when the named query-string parameter equals the exact string 'True' (capital T). Any other value — including 'true', '1', 'yes' — or an absent parameter evaluates off. This is case-sensitive and must be exact.

trigger_keywords: path matches regex anchored or not
how does path condition match
is path condition a full match or substring search
path matches condition behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path-matches condition uses a regex search (substring match), not a full match or anchored match. The condition passes if the pattern is found anywhere within the request path string.

trigger_keywords: invalid date in after date condition
what happens when date string is malformed
after date condition error handling
non-parseable datetime value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the value supplied to the after-date condition is not a valid ISO 8601 datetime string, the condition must evaluate off (result=false) rather than raising an exception or returning an error object.

trigger_keywords: duplicate condition registration
register condition that already exists
what error when condition name is taken
registering built-in condition name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attempting to register a condition under a name that is already taken (including built-in names like 'boolean') must be rejected with a normalized error: `error=duplicate_condition` followed by `condition=<name>`. Built-in conditions are pre-registered and cannot be overwritten.

trigger_keywords: flag source provider failure behavior
what happens when a flag source throws
ignore errors flag source option
source not found error vs source failed error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a provider raises an exception during flag loading, the default output is `error=source_failed`. If `ignore_errors=true` is set, the failing provider is skipped silently and an empty collection is returned (flags=0). If the provider module path cannot be imported at all, the error is `error=source_not_found`.

trigger_keywords: multi-source flag merge order
how are flags combined from multiple providers
flags listing output format from multiple sources
stable ordering of merged flags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When multiple providers are merged, their flags are combined into one collection. The output must list the total flag count across all providers, and flags must appear in a stable (alphabetical) order. Each flag entry shows its condition count followed by each condition's name and value.

trigger_keywords: view guard fallback behavior
what happens when flag is off and fallback is set
guarded view 404 vs fallback
required state polarity inversion in view guard
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A guarded view with required-state=true serves the primary view (status 200, body 'ok') when the flag is on, and returns 404 when the flag is off unless a fallback is configured, in which case the fallback is served (status 200, body 'fallback'). With required-state=false the polarity inverts: disabled/missing flags serve the view, enabled flags are blocked or routed to the fallback.

trigger_keywords: flag disjunction logic
how are multiple conditions on a flag combined
is it AND or OR for flag conditions
any condition passes means flag is on
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A flag's state is the disjunction (OR) of its conditions: the flag is on if ANY single condition passes. It is off only when all conditions are present but none pass. A flag with no conditions configured is off.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'validate_config', 'describe_record', 'check_condition', 'register_condition', 'lookup_condition', 'resolve_flag', 'list_flags', 'guarded_view', 'route', 'request_cache', 'render_django', 'render_jinja', 'submit_form', 'form_choices'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The request-context handling pattern: passing request as optional context to condition evaluation, and the normalized missing_required_argument error contract used when request-dependent conditions are called without a request

context_pointers.vague_reference: static config and whatever storage backend
context_pointers.actual_meaning: SettingsFlagsSource (reads from settings/config dict) and DatabaseFlagsSource (reads from persisted records), both implementing the provider interface and mergeable via the aggregator

context_pointers.vague_reference: validation, admin forms, caching per request
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 (config validation with flags.E001), Feature 9 (FlagForm with condition choices and record persistence), and Feature 7 (middleware-based request-scoped flag caching) respectively

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific details on from the original requirements. I'd suggest checking the existing codebase patterns or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the authoritative contract on that behavior.

feature10_1_config_check.json.description: Configuration validation check: flags are scanned for conditions that name a non-existent condition type. A configuration whose flags reference only known conditions produces no warnings; a flag that references an unregistered condition produces exactly one warning carrying a stable diagnostic id.

feature11_1_record_description.json.description: Flag record human-readable description: a persisted flag record renders to a sentence describing the flag, the condition that enables it, and the value it is enabled at.

cases.expected_output: description=MY_FLAG is enabled when boolean is True

feature1_1_boolean_condition.json.description: Boolean condition: evaluate a literal boolean or its case-insensitive string spelling to a definite on/off result. The string 'true' (any casing) and the literal true evaluate on; everything else evaluates off.

feature1_2_user_condition.json.description: User condition: passes when the current request's user has the expected username. Requires request context; when the request is absent the evaluation reports a normalized missing-required-argument error naming the missing argument and the condition.

feature1_3_anonymous_condition.json.description: Anonymous condition: compares whether the request's user is anonymous against an expected boolean. Passes when the anonymity of the user matches the expected boolean. Requires request context; without a request it reports a normalized missing-required-argument error.

feature1_4_parameter_condition.json.description: Parameter condition: passes when a named query-string parameter on the request equals the exact string 'True'. Any other value, or an absent parameter, evaluates off. Requires request context; without a request it reports a normalized missing-required-argument error.

feature1_5_path_condition.json.description: Path-matches condition: passes when the request path contains a match for the given regular expression anywhere within it (substring/regex search, not anchored). Requires request context; without a request it reports a normalized missing-required-argument error.

feature1_6_date_condition.json.description: After-date condition: passes when the current moment is at or after the given ISO 8601 datetime. A datetime far in the past evaluates on; a datetime far in the future evaluates off; a string that is not a valid datetime evaluates off rather than failing.

input.value: I am not a valid date

feature2_1_register_condition.json.description: Condition registry registration: a new condition can be registered under a name and is then discoverable in the registry. Registering a name that is already taken is rejected with a normalized duplicate-condition error naming the condition.

feature2_2_lookup_condition.json.description: Condition registry lookup: looking up a built-in condition name reports it as found, while looking up a name that was never registered reports it as not found.

feature3_1_flag_state.json.description: Flag state resolution: resolving a flag returns its combined condition result. A flag whose conditions are all satisfied resolves on; a flag whose conditions are unsatisfied resolves off; a flag that is not configured resolves to a distinct none/undefined value (not the same as off).

feature3_2_flag_enabled.json.description: Enabled helper: a convenience query that reports whether a flag is currently on. An enabled flag reports on; a disabled flag reports off.

feature3_3_flag_disabled.json.description: Disabled helper: a convenience query that reports the negation of a flag's state. A disabled flag reports on; an enabled flag reports off.

feature4_1_settings_source.json.description: Settings-backed flag provider: reads flag definitions from the static configuration mapping and exposes each flag with its conditions. The output lists the total flag count and, per flag, each condition's name and value.

cases.expected_output:
flags=1
flag=MY_FLAG conditions=1
condition flag=MY_FLAG name=boolean value=True

feature4_2_database_source.json.description: Database-backed flag provider: reads persisted flag records and exposes each as a flag with one condition carrying the stored condition name and value. The output lists the total flag count and the per-flag condition detail.

cases.expected_output:
flags=1
flag=MY_FLAG conditions=1
condition flag=MY_FLAG name=boolean value=False

feature4_3_merge_sources.json.description: Multi-provider merge: flags from several providers are combined into one collection. A flag defined in the static configuration with no conditions appears alongside flags supplied by an additional provider, and a flag only the extra provider defines is included. The output lists the merged flag count with each flag's condition count and detail.

cases.expected_output:
flags=3
flag=NOT_IN_SETTINGS_FLAG conditions=1
condition flag=NOT_IN_SETTINGS_FLAG name=boolean value=False
flag=OTHER_FLAG conditions=0
flag=SOURCED_FLAG conditions=1
condition flag=SOURCED_FLAG name=boolean value=True

feature4_4_source_errors.json.description: Provider error handling: when a provider fails while producing flags, the default behavior surfaces a normalized source-failure error. When errors are explicitly ignored, the failing provider is skipped and an empty collection is returned. A provider path that cannot be located surfaces a normalized source-not-found error.

feature5_1_function_view_guard.json.description: Function view access guard: a view is wrapped so it is served only when a flag matches a required state. With required-state on: an enabled flag serves the view (status 200, body 'ok'); a disabled or missing flag is blocked (status 404) unless a fallback view is supplied, in which case the fallback is served (status 200, body 'fallback'). With required-state off, the polarity inverts: a disabled or missing flag serves the view while an enabled flag is blocked or routed to the fallback.

feature5_2_class_view_guard.json.description: Class-based view access guard: a class view is gated on a flag the same way. When the gating flag is enabled the view responds (status 200, body 'ok'); when disabled or missing it is blocked (status 404) unless a fallback view is configured, in which case the fallback responds. If the view is configured without naming a flag at all, it reports a normalized misconfiguration error.

feature6_1_single_route.json.description: Single flag-guarded route: a route is bound to a flag and a required state. Resolving and invoking the route returns the primary view (status 200, body 'view') when the flag matches the required state; otherwise it returns not-found (status 404) when no fallback is registered, or the fallback view (status 200, body 'fallback') when one is. Required-state may be off, inverting the polarity.

feature6_2_included_routes.json.description: Flag-guarded included route groups: an entire group of routes can be mounted under a prefix and guarded by a single flag. When the flag matches, requests to a member route serve the primary view; when it does not match, they return not-found. A group mounted with required-state off serves its members when the flag is off. When the guard has a fallback group whose members mirror the primary routes, a non-matching flag serves the corresponding fallback for matching member paths and for members that exist only in the fallback group, while paths absent from both yield not-found.

feature6_3_bulk_routes.json.description: Bulk route guarding via a shared context: multiple routes declared together inherit one flag, required-state, and optional fallback. Members behave like single guarded routes: a route declared with required-state on serves the primary view when the flag is on and is not-found when off; a route declared with required-state off serves the primary view when the flag is off; a route declared with a shared fallback serves the fallback when the flag does not match the required state.

feature6_4_invalid_view.json.description: Invalid route target rejection: attempting to build a flag-guarded route whose target view is neither a callable nor a route group is rejected with a normalized invalid-view error.

feature7_1_request_cache.json.description: Request-scoped flag caching: middleware attaches the resolved flag collection to the request so later checks reuse it. A request that has not passed through the middleware carries no cached attribute; after the middleware runs, the attribute is present and lists the configured flag names. Once cached, a flag check against that request keeps using the cached snapshot even if the live configuration is emptied afterward; a request without the cached snapshot reflects the now-empty configuration instead.

feature8_1_template_tags.json.description: Server-side template flag tags: a template can branch on flag state using an enabled tag and a disabled tag, each assigning a boolean into a template variable. The enabled tag yields a truthy value for an on flag and falsy for an off or missing flag; the disabled tag is its inverse. An extra keyword argument supplied at the tag call is forwarded to the flag's condition so value-matching conditions can pass. Rendering 'on' or 'off' shows the branch taken.

feature8_2_jinja_tags.json.description: Alternative template-engine flag globals: a second template engine exposes enabled and disabled flag functions that return a rendered boolean. The enabled function renders 'True' for an on flag and 'False' for an off flag; the disabled function is its inverse. A keyword argument passed in the call is forwarded to the flag's condition so value-matching conditions can pass.

feature9_1_form_validation.json.description: Flag-state form validation and persistence: a form accepts a flag name, a condition name, and a value. Valid submissions persist a flag record and echo the saved name, condition, and value. Blank submissions are rejected, reporting which required fields are invalid.

feature9_2_form_choices.json.description: Late-bound condition choices: the form's condition choices are computed when the form is built, not when it is defined, so a condition registered after import is still offered. Querying a freshly registered condition reports it as present among the choices.
